pattern: At each tile you either leave it exposed or spend one carpet to blanket the next carpetLen tiles starting there, so the visible-white-tile count is a straightforward take-or-skip decision chained left to right, which is exactly what dynamic programming tracks.
hints: Process the floor left to right; at each position you have exactly two choices - leave the current tile uncovered, or lay a carpet starting here that covers the next carpetLen tiles in one go.

Let dp[i][c] be the minimum visible white tiles among the first i tiles using at most c carpets; either dp[i-1][c] plus this tile's own color (skip), or dp[max(0,i-carpetLen)][c-1] (cover this tile and the carpetLen-1 before it with one carpet).

Carpets only ever need to be placed so their right edge lines up with some tile, so you never need to consider a carpet start offset by anything other than exactly carpetLen back from the current position.

edge_inputs.description: carpets that can overlap to clear all but two tiles
enough carpet coverage to clear the whole floor
single tile, single carpet at least as long as the floor
carpetLen equal to floor length, one carpet covers everything at once
far more carpets available than needed, extra carpets must not be forced to be used
floor is entirely black already, answer is zero regardless of carpets
zero carpets worth of budget is never given, but one single carpet must still be optimally placed rather than defaulting to the first tiles

stress.description: 300-tile random floor with a moderate carpet budget and length, exercising the DP transition boundaries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    floor = ''.join(rng.choice('01') for _ in range(n))
    carpet_len = rng.randrange(1, n + 1)
    num_carpets = rng.randrange(1, min(n, 50) + 1)
    return [floor, num_carpets, carpet_len]
stress.note: carpetLen bounded by floor length as required; moderate size keeps the O(n * numCarpets) oracle fast
